Pet Life Log
Ask Scout
Trusted sources only
Bailey's been drinking a lot more lately.
Since Bailey has kidney disease, increased thirst is something the sources flag as worth watching.This isn't a diagnosis — increased thirst can be serious. Check with your vet.

Built by a vet

Made by a practicing veterinarian.

Pet Life Log tracks and informs — it never diagnoses or replaces your vet.

Tracks & informs.
Never diagnoses.

Built by a practicing veterinarian, for the pets who need a little extra keeping track of.

A closer look at everything inside.

Built for the pets who need a little extra keeping track of — here's what that means, feature by feature.

Vet-visit summary

One tap turns a month of notes into a vet-ready page.

The thing this whole app is built around: walk into the exam room with everything organized, instead of trying to remember it.

What's on it

  • Opens with conditions and allergies — the frame your vet reads everything else through.
  • Weight direction over the period, at a glance.
  • Every dose given, late, or skipped.
  • Symptom frequency plus appetite, water, and energy patterns.
  • Specialized logs in range — seizure counts, resting breaths, water intake.
  • Pinned lab trends, the calorie target, and any active elimination trial.

You stay in control

  • Every section toggles on or off before you share (quality-of-life stays private by default).
  • The span defaults to since your last visit — change it anytime.
  • Your running questions for the vet fold right in.

Send it your way

  • Export a clean PDF to print, email, or text.
  • Call to book or share to the clinic from the same screen.

Medications

Never lose track of a dose.

The daily workhorse — built so a busy household covers every dose between them, and a missed one never quietly slips.

Daily doses

  • Reminders for every dose on a flexible schedule.
  • Mark a dose given right from the notification.
  • Multi-phase tapers built in — the schedule advances on its own.
  • As-needed meds: one tap records who gave it and when.

When something's missed

  • A missed dose pings the whole household, not just one person.
  • Anyone can tap "I've got it" to claim it.
  • One-tap undo if you mis-tapped — a wrong "given" could mean a double dose.

Refills, info & safety

  • Counts down each dose and warns about a week before you run out.
  • A "recheck before refill" flag when your pet's due for a visit.
  • Tap any med for a plain-language page: what it does and side effects to watch — and nothing on dosing, on purpose.
Dosing always comes from your vet — the app never sets or changes a dose.

Daily diary & trackers

Log a weight, a symptom, or a note in seconds.

Low-effort by design — because the log only helps if it actually gets filled in on a normal day.

Quick everyday logging

  • A dated note with optional structured tags.
  • Tap appetite, water, or energy without typing.
  • Dictate notes and symptoms with your voice.

Trackers, your way

  • Weight trends with weekly or monthly reminders.
  • The diary is also your trackers hub — each thing you follow is a tile.
  • Build your own tracker for anything, with its own history.
  • Trackers switch on automatically for your pet's conditions.

Specialized trackers

The numbers that actually change, tracked simply.

Purpose-built logs for the things chronic and senior pets need watched — each one its own screen and its own trend.

Resting breaths

  • Tap once per breath over 15 or 30 seconds — the app does the math.
  • A watch-number (commonly 30) with a gentle nudge if readings climb.
  • Best taken while your pet's asleep or calm.

Seizures

  • Log time, duration, type, severity, recovery, and a suspected trigger.
  • Builds a frequency trend automatically.
  • Attach a short video of an event.

Water, quality of life & recovery

  • Water intake: a measured amount plus a quick "more / less than usual."
  • Quality of life: plain sub-scores trended over time, with a gentle prompt on a sustained decline.
  • Post-op recovery: daily check-ins through the healing window, then it closes.

Records & scanning

Snap it once, filed forever.

The pile of paperwork from every visit, turned into something you can actually find and use.

Scan anything

  • Photograph bloodwork or vaccine records — or import a photo or PDF.
  • The app reads it and fills in the details, side by side with the original.
  • Anything it can't read is left blank and flagged for you.

Filed and useful

  • Sorted into Bloodwork, Vaccines, Receipts, and Other.
  • A scanned vaccine offers to set its next-due reminder.
  • Lab numbers feed straight into your condition trackers.

Video clips

  • Attach a short (60-second) clip to the timeline or a seizure entry.
  • Record in-app or import one you already filmed.

Conditions & lab values

Understand the diagnosis, watch the trend.

Make sense of what your pet's been diagnosed with — and actually see the numbers move over time.

Plain-language condition pages

  • What it is, likely causes, signs to watch, and when to call.
  • What's typically used to manage it — in plain terms, never dosing.
  • Works for common conditions and ones you type in yourself.

Lab values that mean something

  • Reports as dated tabs — open one to see that panel, never an endless list.
  • The app suggests which values to follow for a condition.
  • They're grouped into one tracker by organ system — "Kidney" = Creatinine, BUN, SDMA, Phosphorus.
  • Graphed against the reference range, with the trend front and center.
  • Scan-only, so a typo never corrupts the trend.

Care reminders & contacts

Nothing slips through.

Every routine thing on the calendar — plus the weather-aware nudges that matter for an anxious pet.

Preventive care

  • Vaccines, heartworm, flea and tick — set by hand, by interval, or from a scan.
  • Appointment types get a "Call to book" button.

Weather-aware calm

  • Storm and fireworks nudges, triggered by the forecast or a date you set.
  • Staggered lead-time reminders you control.
  • One tap to give the calming med.

Your vet, one tap away

  • Save your regular vet, ER, urgent care, and specialists.
  • Find vets and ERs near you, or add them by hand.
  • Tap to call from anywhere in the app.

Feeding

How much to feed, in your pet's actual food.

No more guessing at "a cup or two." Scan the bag and get a number you can trust — then go deeper for the tricky cases.

Get the right portion

  • Scan your food's nutrition label — camera, photo, or PDF.
  • A daily calorie target worked out for your specific pet.
  • The portion in your actual food — real cups or grams of your bag.
  • Adjusts for weight, life stage and activity, and spay/neuter.
  • Flows into your vet-visit summary.

Running an elimination diet

  • Log every food tried; get suggested untried proteins.
  • A structured 8–12 week trial with reintroduction steps.
  • A daily "any slip-ups?" check so a broken trial is recorded, not hidden.

Cooking at home, safely

  • A link to board-certified veterinary nutritionists for complete, balanced recipes.
A rough guide. Your pet may have different needs, especially with certain medical conditions — check with your vet.

Meet Scout

Ask anything about your pet — and actually get an answer.

Scout is the app's helper, a tap away on every screen. Ask him to explain a diagnosis, talk through symptoms, or just find where something lives.

Understand what's going on

  • "What is diabetes?" — Scout explains the condition in plain English: what it is, what causes it, and the signs to watch.
  • Go as deep as you want — symptoms, how it's generally managed, what a lab value actually means.
  • Only from trusted veterinary sources, never the open internet.
  • Answers come in context — "since Bailey has kidney disease…" — using what he already knows about your pet.

Find anything in the app

  • "Where do I log a seizure?" — Scout takes you straight there.
  • Pinned to every screen, so he's one tap away from anywhere.
  • Keep asking follow-ups in the same thread.
For anything urgent, Scout leads with a one-tap link to your ER — and for the calls only your vet should make, he points you to them. Scout informs; he never diagnoses.
Scout
What is diabetes in dogs?
In short: the body can't move sugar from the blood into its cells for energy, usually because there's too little insulin. Common signs are drinking and peeing more, weight loss, and a bigger appetite.
Signs to watch →How it's managed →

Family & sitter sharing

Everyone on the same page — even the pet sitter.

Care rarely falls on one person. Keep the whole household — and whoever's watching your pet — in sync.

Your household

  • Each family member gets their own login.
  • Everyone sees who gave what, and when.
  • You edit your own entries; the owner can edit anyone's.

Handing off to a sitter

  • Share a simple link — no account, no app to install.
  • They follow the feeding plan and give the day's meds with one big tap.
  • They log what they see from a tap-menu (ate, didn't eat, vomited…) — no typing.
  • Big buttons to call you, the vet, or the ER.

You stay in the loop

  • Sitter entries sync to your timeline live, tagged "Sitter."
  • When the link expires, their access ends but their notes stay.
